cluster_name: Thai Princess Bajrakitiyabha dies after years-long coma
event_summary: Thailand's Princess Bajrakitiyabha Mahidol, 47, died on Thursday evening June 11, 2026, at a Bangkok hospital from an abdominal infection after nearly four years in a coma following a sudden illness in December 2022.
tldr: Headlines across 22 outlets overwhelmingly foreground Princess Bajrakitiyabha's years-long illness and coma as the defining context of her death, with phrases like 'after years in coma', 'after long illness', and 'three years in hospital' appearing in at least 15 of 22 titles. The mourning layer — emotional grief and national ritual — is present only in three outliers: Geo News uses conspicuously emotional language ('heartbreaking loss', 'beloved Princess'), News24 leads with a eulogy fragment, and South China Morning Post is the sole outlet to headline the 15-day national mourning period rather than the death itself. The princess's professional identity as a lawyer, UN advocate, and diplomat — prominent in body text — is almost entirely absent from headlines, with Republic World the sole exception naming her 'UN Advocate' in the title.

frames.sub_frame: prolonged coma and illness as context

evidence.quote: dies at 47 following years-long coma, Royal Palace says
Thai princess Bajrakitiyabha dies after years in coma
king's eldest daughter dies, aged 47, after long illness
heartbreaking loss as beloved Princess dies

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline in the corpus to use conspicuously emotional language ('heartbreaking loss', 'beloved Princess') — a register absent from all other 21 titles.
Only headline to foreground the 15-day national mourning period rather than the death or illness — the sole outlet to lead with royal ceremonial response.
Only headline to name her as 'UN Advocate' — foregrounding her professional identity over royal status or health narrative.
Reports age as 48 in the headline — the only outlet to diverge from the 47 given by the palace and every other outlet.
Unique interrogative headline ('De qué murió' — What did she die of) positions the title as a medical inquiry, the only headline to foreground the cause rather than the fact of death.
Only headline to embed a eulogy fragment ('She was loved, respected and admired') directly in the title — an unusual construction absent from all other outlets in this corpus.

bottom_line: The headline layer of this story is dominated by a uniform health-narrative frame — nearly every title across 11 of 13 country groups signals the years-long coma or illness as the essential context of the death, producing rare cross-ideological convergence. The mourning and professional-legacy dimensions that distinguish body-text coverage are headline silences, surfaced only by three outliers (Geo News, News24, SCMP) and invisible to a reader who sees only the front page.
